### Adaptation in Digital Journalism

In the rapidly evolving landscape of digital journalism, adaptation has become a crucial strategy for survival and growth. This special issue of _Digital Journalism_ seeks to explore the multifaceted nature of adaptation within the field, examining how the relevant actors and institutions of digital journalism proactively and reactively adapt to technological advancements, shifting audience behaviors, and the changing socio-political environment.

As a construct that has emerged out of biology, anthropology, and health sciences, we know that adaptation is crucial human skill. Yet as Sarta et al. (2021) argue, “scholars have used the concept of adaptation inconsistently across research traditions without always being able to push the research agenda beyond analogical reasoning” (p. 44). While there might be a notion that adaptation is a passive process, one that happens to, for example, journalists or journalism organizations, this is only one portion of the concept. Research primarily defines adaptation as a response or reaction to a force in that an “instance of adaptation is viewed as a modification” that occurs “in reaction (or response, for that matter) to an external or environmental contravention” (Sachs & Meditz, 1979, p. 1084; Giddens, 1999). In this way, adaptation is opportunistic and describes how an individual or organization or institution can choose change and but still engage in a range of different forms of adaptation (Sachs & Meditz, 1979). Adaptation in digital journalism can take many forms, from the integration of emerging technologies and platforms to the reimagining of practices and ethics. And there are a range of actors engaged in the adaptation in digital journalism, who may not be formally affiliate with _journalism_, and who conduct work relevant to the overall adaptation of the field (as with technologists, peripheral actors).

In our field, adaptation has been primarily considered through the lens of technology, yet the actors of digital journalism actively adapt to a range of actions, actors and contexts: changes in the audience (e.g. rising audience hostility), physical environment (e.g. COVID protocols, violence), personal circumstances (e.g. precarity, life changes, employment disillusionment), political environment (e.g. democratic backsliding), market changes, and others. Adaptation means actors at times engage in “adoption” of new processes, seeking to normalize them as a part of working routines (Perreault & Ferrucci, 2020). As actors have engaged in platformization, this means at times that they have adapted through the _stacking_ of platform-specific skills, using the skills gained in adapting to one platform to jumpstart their adaptation to others. But at times actors also engage in “selection” of other processes to denormalise when they no longer serve (e.g. many journalists are stepping away from social media; Bossio et al., 2024). Research produced within the “emotional turn” (e.g. Wahl-Jorgensen, 2020) and “audience turn” (e.g. Costera Meijer, 2020) shows that, to stay relevant to changing audiences and new political and cultural contexts, actors reconsider/select old and actively adopt new processes and skills. For example, journalists have engaged in adaptation through personalizing their reporting, using authenticity, empathy, and passion as strategic skills, building emotional and trauma literacy, and redefining long-dominating cornerstones of journalistic professionalism, such as objectivity and impartiality.

Digital journalism bears meaningful similarities in this regard to other fields: journalists can anticipate change even if they don’t know what that change will entail. But conversely, and unlike other fields, journalists are often not provided the resources to ease adaptation. For this reason, this special issue seeks to center _adaptability_ as a crucial journalistic professional skill; it is perhaps more crucial in journalism than other fields given that journalists consistently find themselves negotiating new circumstances and environments as a native part of their work.

This special issue invites contributions that investigate these adaptive processes, particularly those that challenge traditional norms and propose innovative approaches to journalism in the digital age.

We are interested in a wide and overlapping range of digital journalism actors–journalists, technologists, businesspeople, fact checkers, fixers, peripheral actors, news organizations, platforms, policymakers, regulatory bodies–and topics, including but not limited to:

●**Technological Adaptation**: How are relevant actors and organizations incorporating emerging technologies such as AI, VR, and blockchain into their workflows? What are the implications of these technologies for media integrity and audience trust? How have actors adapted through platformization and datafication?

●**Emotional Adaptation**: How are the actors of digital journalism adapting emotionally to changes within the media ecosystem to which they can have little effect? What are the means by which actors engage in selection in order to engage in emotional management? How can actors cultivate and actively employ emotional literacy to adapt to changing media landscapes and audience behavior, increase their relevance for broader audiences, and secure their unique role and place within the attention economy?

●**Adaptation to Audiences**: How are the actors of digital journalism adapting to changes in audience behavior and preferences? What strategies are being employed to engage diverse and fragmented audiences? How can actors actively go to meet their audiences, including young audiences, where they are?

●**Normative Adaptation**: How are ethical standards in media being redefined in the digital era? What new ethical dilemmas are emerging, and how are the actors of digital journalism addressing them? How have norms adapted to digitization?

●**Economic Adaptation**: How are news and tech organizations adapting their business models to ensure sustainability in a digital-first world? What innovative revenue streams are being explored?

●**Cultural and Political Adaptation**: How are the actors of digital journalism navigating the complex cultural and political landscapes of the 21st century? How are they addressing issues of misinformation, polarization, and censorship?

Extended abstracts should include an abstract of 500 words (not including references) as well as a full list of author(s) with affiliation(s) and abbreviated bio(s). Please submit your proposal to Dr. Gregory Perreault (gperreault@usf.edu) as one file (PDF) with your names clearly stated on the first page.

Full manuscripts should target a length of 7,000-9,000 words.

# How Journalism is Adapting to the Digital Age – From Print to Podcasts

### Transformation of Journalism

![People doing podcast](https://greyjournal.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Untitled-design-2024-10-02T012725.186.jpg)
![Grace Hawkins](https://greyjournal.net/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/Pic.jpg)
![GREY Journal](https://greyjournal.net/wp-content/uploads/2019/09/WEB3_970x70_AnimatedBanner_2-1.gif)

The rapid rise of digital technology has transformed countless industries, and journalism is no exception. Once dominated by print media, the landscape of news reporting has shifted dramatically to accommodate new formats such as online news, podcasts, and social media updates. While the digital age brings opportunities for wider reach and more dynamic content, it also challenges traditional journalism’s business models and methods. In this evolving environment, understanding how to navigate the changes is essential for both journalists and readers alike.

This transition parallels the marketing world, where businesses are turning to digital strategies such as [SEO for CBD companies](https://cbdseoxperts.com/) to reach their audiences. Just as businesses must adopt new tactics to stay competitive, journalism must embrace digital tools to continue delivering credible and timely information to the public.

## **The Decline of Print Journalism**

In the early 2000s, print journalism began to experience a significant decline. Daily newspapers, once a primary news source for millions, started losing readership and advertising revenue to digital competitors. The internet offered faster, more convenient ways to consume news, and print media couldn’t keep up with the 24-hour news cycle that digital platforms enabled. Furthermore, the shift in advertising dollars from print to digital channels has made it harder for newspapers to sustain their operations.

Traditional media houses have had to rethink their models as digital competitors, including online news outlets, social media, and even blogs, became the go-to sources for news. The financial challenges facing the print industry led to the closure of numerous local newspapers and a reduction in print editions for many national publications.

## **The Rise of Digital Journalism**

![](https://greyjournal.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Untitled-design-2024-10-02T020346.662-1024x475.jpg)

Digital journalism has opened up new ways of reaching audiences that print media could never achieve. Websites, social media, and email newsletters have made news more accessible, allowing people to access articles, videos, and reports in real-time from anywhere in the world.

One of the significant advantages of digital journalism is its multimedia capabilities. Articles are no longer limited to text and images. Journalists can now include interactive graphics, embedded videos, live streams, and data visualizations, making news stories more dynamic and engaging. Readers can dive deeper into topics through hyperlinks and connected stories, creating a more immersive experience.

Additionally, digital platforms allow for instant feedback and engagement through comments, shares, and likes. News organizations can use social media to gauge public interest in stories, interact with readers, and build a more loyal audience base.

However, with the rise of digital media also came the challenge of misinformation and “fake news.” Social media, where information can spread rapidly without verification, has caused issues for the credibility of digital journalism. News organizations are investing more in fact-checking and transparency to maintain trust with their audiences.

## **Podcasts: A New Era of Storytelling**

One of the most exciting developments in journalism over the past decade is the rise of podcasts. Podcasts, which are audio programs that can be streamed or downloaded online, offer a unique platform for in-depth storytelling, interviews, and analysis.

Unlike traditional news articles, which are often consumed quickly, podcasts provide an opportunity for longer-form journalism. They allow journalists to explore stories in more detail and engage listeners on a deeper level. For instance, investigative podcasts like *Serial* have captivated millions with their episodic storytelling, shining a light on complex, unsolved cases.

Podcasts are especially appealing because they offer flexibility in how they are consumed. Listeners can tune in while commuting, exercising, or relaxing, making it easier for people to integrate journalism into their busy lives. The success of podcasts like *The Daily* by *The New York Times* has shown that there is a large audience for audio journalism.

The monetization of podcasts has also opened up new revenue streams for news organizations, helping to offset the losses from print and online ad revenue. Sponsorships and advertisements in popular podcasts provide a viable way for journalists to fund their work and continue creating quality content.

## **Video Journalism and the Role of Social Media**

![](https://greyjournal.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Untitled-design-2024-10-02T020244.125-1024x475.jpg)

In addition to podcasts, video journalism has become a critical part of how news is consumed today. With the growing popularity of video content on platforms like YouTube, Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok, news organizations are focusing more on producing short, digestible video segments. These videos often appeal to younger audiences who prefer watching news rather than reading it.

Live streaming is another powerful tool in the digital journalist’s toolkit. News outlets can broadcast events in real-time, giving viewers unfiltered access to breaking news and live coverage of important events. This type of reporting, which is impossible for print media, offers immediacy and immersion that resonates with today’s audiences.

Social media has become an indispensable platform for distributing news. Journalists use Twitter to share breaking news and updates, Facebook to engage with broader audiences, and Instagram to share visual stories. Social media’s algorithm-driven nature, however, can pose challenges in terms of spreading misinformation and sensationalism. Nonetheless, it offers an effective way for journalists to reach wider audiences and keep readers informed in real-time.

## **The Importance of Adapting to Change**

The future of journalism is one of continuous adaptation. Just as newsrooms have shifted from print to digital formats, they must continue to evolve to meet the demands of emerging technologies and changing consumer behaviors.

Subscription models, paywalls, and membership programs are becoming more common as news organizations seek to diversify their revenue streams. With advertising dollars no longer being as dependable as they were in print, news outlets are focusing more on creating high-quality, subscriber-only content to ensure their financial survival. This shift mirrors the challenges faced by industries like e-commerce and marketing, where businesses must use strategies such as **SEO for CBD companies** to attract and retain customers in a competitive online space.

News organizations are also exploring new ways to engage audiences through newsletters, personalized content recommendations, and interactive journalism. The goal is to create experiences that keep readers coming back for more, whether through a podcast, a long-form investigative article, or a live video stream.

## Final Thoughts

The digital age has reshaped journalism in ways that were unimaginable a few decades ago. Print newspapers may have experienced a decline, but digital platforms, podcasts, and video journalism have created new opportunities for storytelling and audience engagement. While the industry faces challenges such as misinformation and shifting revenue models, it also has the tools to adapt and thrive.

Just as businesses must evolve with technology to remain competitive, journalism must continue embracing digital innovations while maintaining its core values of truth, accuracy, and integrity. As we move further into the digital age, one thing is certain: journalism will remain a crucial part of informing and educating society, no matter the format.

Digital news business models 2025: Adapting to AI, search, and creator trends
=============================================================================

By

José Mauricio Duque

Last updated

February 5, 2025

![Image 4: A stylized image of a woman](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6155ab6c75f4f8744b419dab/67a446a503aa6c2bc28f327a_250123%20Tech%20trends%20in%20media%202025.0.jpg)

The Reuters Institute's report, "Journalism, media, and technology trends and predictions 2025," highlights the challenges media outlets will face in the coming years, including increasing hostility toward journalists, changes in search visibility, and the impact of generative AI. Despite these hurdles, the report underscores the need for media organizations to innovate and adapt to thrive in an evolving landscape.

Table of Contents
-----------------

[An increasingly hostile environment for journalists](https://www.newstex.com/blog/digital-news-business-models-2025-adapting-to-ai-search-and-creator-trends#an-increasingly-hostile-environment-for-journalists)

The Reuters Institute for the Study of Journalism at the University of Oxford recently published a fascinating report entitled “[Journalism, media, and technology trends and predictions 2025](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#header--1).” It presents a deep dive into the myriad challenges that media outlets will face this year. In this post, we’ll look at some of the issues they highlighted, including:

*   Hostile politicians;
*   Changes to search results;
*   Generative AI; and
*   The increasing prominence of creators as purveyors of factual information.

An increasingly hostile environment for journalists
---------------------------------------------------

![Image 5: A data visualization illustrating the decline in confidence in journalism from 2022 to 2025, with 2022 at 60% (yellow) and 2025 at 41% (orange), suggesting decreasing trust in the industry over time.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6155ab6c75f4f8744b419dab/67a4de90ac0e0aafff2034f6_Confidence.png)

_Percentages of media leaders who feel confident about the future of journalism._

_‍_ The report revealed that many publishers feel marked uncertainty about the future of journalism. While 60% of media leaders reported feeling confident about the future of their industry in 2022, that figure [had decreased](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#header--1) to 41% in 2025. Freedom of the press has increasingly come under attack. More and more countries are passing ‘foreign agent’ laws similar to those already on the books in Russia. Passed in 2022, the Russian law allows a wide variety of individuals and organizations to be classified as ‘foreign agents’ simply because the authorities claim they are somehow under ‘foreign influence.’

According to Human Rights Watch, foreign agents face [a number of restrictions](https://www.hrw.org/news/2022/12/01/russia-new-restrictions-foreign-agents), including bans on creating educational material for minors or organizing public assemblies. And Reporters Without Borders has shown that [media outlets make up a third of all foreign agents](https://rsf.org/en/russia-independent-media-are-primary-targets-kremlin-laws-against-foreign-agents-and-undesirable), which has stifled independent journalism in Russia.Even in the US, where freedom of the press is guaranteed by the Constitution, it’s far from smooth sailing.

> It’s telling that two of the most high-profile interviews of the 2024 presidential campaign weren’t conducted by traditional journalists but by influential podcasters such as Joe Rogan and Alex Cooper.”

Donald Trump is currently [suing the _Des Moines Register_](https://www.desmoinesregister.com/story/news/crime-and-courts/2024/12/18/trump-lawsuit-des-moines-register-gannett-iowa-election-poll-federal-court/77066847007/) after they published a poll claiming that Kamala Harris had a 3-point lead over Trump in Iowa. He’s also [suing the Pulitzer Center](https://www.politico.com/news/2024/07/21/trump-libel-suit-pulitzer-prize-00169975) for awarding their namesake prize to the _New York Times_ and the _Washington Post_ for a series of reports on links between the Trump campaign and Russia in 2016. Although they are only civil proceedings, they are still a threat to these organizations since they must spend considerable amounts of money to defend themselves in court.

There’s also a real risk that [Trump’s incessant verbal attacks on the media](https://rsf.org/en/usa-trump-verbally-attacked-media-more-100-times-run-election) could nudge his supporters into acts of violence, such as when [a Colorado man allegedly attacked a reporter](https://apnews.com/article/tv-reporter-attack-trumps-america-40c4c3622bf85baef9cefbd50d50fcec) while claiming “[t]his is Trump’s America now.”Visibility problemsActually getting their content in front of eyeballs is becoming more and more challenging. Platforms such as X and Facebook were once major sources of referral traffic, but that trend is moving in the wrong direction.

![Image 6](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6155ab6c75f4f8744b419dab/67a4e1a3a9ae396f4350b5c6_Pros%20and%20cons.png)

The Reuters Institute’s report showed a staggering decline, with [traffic to news and media properties declining](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#header--2) by two-thirds on Facebook and half on X over the last two years. The situation on Google is not as dire, but its decision to prominently feature AI-generated answers at the top of the page presents a different challenge. Although they feature citations that readers can click through to see the information that the AI drew from when coming up with the answer, many in the media are understandably worried about future click-through rates. If the AI appears to have answered their question, many people will simply move on without consulting the sources.

![Image 7: A flowchart-style infographic illustrating ways to adapt to declining referral traffic. The title is centered at the top. Three arrows branch out—left (blue) for diversifying platforms, right (green) for enhancing AI engagement, and downward (orange) for investing in direct traffic](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6155ab6c75f4f8744b419dab/67a4e031d76ef119eb1182a8_How%20to%20addapt.png)

The challenges and opportunities of AI
--------------------------------------

Generative AI is one of the biggest challenges facing the established media. Some have tried to contend with it by officially licensing their content to generative AI developers such as when News Corp [signed a $250 million licensing deal with OpenAI](https://pressgazette.co.uk/platforms/news-publisher-ai-deals-lawsuits-openai-google/). But such deals only benefit one particular segment of the media ecosystem. As Rasmus Kleis Nielsen has pointed out, the primary beneficiaries so far have tended to be [well-established English-language publications](https://www.niemanlab.org/2024/12/publishers-find-the-ai-era-not-all-that-lucrative/). News Corp, for example, owns such venerable titles as the _Wall Street Journal_ and _The Times_ (London).

> These challenges underscore the need for media organizations to adapt and innovate in order to survive and thrive.

There can be other pitfalls with AI. Apple recently had to [suspend their AI-generated news alerts](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cq5ggew08eyo) after it repeatedly produced inaccurate results, such as when a faulty summary of BBC news headlines resulted in the AI telling readers that [Luigi Mangione had shot himself](https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cd0elzk24dno). This was a nightmare scenario for the BBC. Since the alerts didn’t say they were made with AI and prominently featured the BBC’s logo, there was a real risk of their credibility being damaged.It’s not all doom and gloom, however. The Reuters Institute’s report found that AI can be beneficial within the newsroom. Generative AI can [aid journalists in a number of ways](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#header--8), including newsgathering and data verification. It can also assist with lower-level content creation tasks such as producing summaries and headlines and basic editorial functions.

The creatorification of media
-----------------------------

![Image 8: An infographic titled ‘Influence of social media on news consumption’ contains three labeled circular charts. From left to right: 21% of overall Americans (yellow), 37% of Americans under 30 (orange), and 77% of influencers without a media background (red). Each percentage is shown as a filled segment in a donut chart.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6155ab6c75f4f8744b419dab/67a4e1d17a8665fb0594d3f2_News%20consumption.png)

_Percentage of respondents who said they get their news from social media influencers._

It’s telling that two of the most high-profile interviews of the 2024 presidential campaign weren’t conducted by traditional journalists. Rather, they were the work of influential podcasters such as Joe Rogan and Alex Cooper. Both the Republicans and the Democrats invited social media influencers to cover their conventions. Data from the Pew Research Center helps [explain this state of affairs](https://www.pewresearch.org/journalism/2024/11/18/americas-news-influencers/), as they found that 21% of Americans say they get their news from influencers on social media (a figure that rises to 37% with people under 30). This fact is made even more remarkable by the fact that the vast majority of these influencers (77% to be precise) have no background in, or connections with, traditional media outlets. The Reuters Institute found that [similar trends can be observed across the globe](https://reutersinstitute.politics.ox.ac.uk/journalism-media-and-technology-trends-and-predictions-2025#header--5).

> The Reuters Institute’s report showed a staggering decline, with traffic to news and media properties declining by two-thirds on Facebook and half on X over the last two years

At the same time, people who do have a formal background in journalism have been tempted to jump ship and rebrand themselves as influencers. It’s not hard to see why when someone like Matthew Yglesias is allegedly making [$1 million a year from Substack alone](https://www.businessinsider.com/matt-yglesias-substack-million-dollars-interview-2024-10). Another journalist-turned-influencer, ex-_Washington Post_ reporter Taylor Lorenz, had this to say about her decision to switch gears: “I don’t need [legacy media] for credibility,” she [told the _New Yorker_](https://www.newyorker.com/culture/infinite-scroll/taylor-lorenzs-plan-to-dance-on-legacy-medias-grave). “I don’t need it to reach an audience. I don’t know what it does other than connote prestige for a shrinking amount of people. Legacy media sucks, it’s crumbling, and, by the way, I’m going to dance on the grave of a lot of these places.”The freedom that comes from this approach can be a double-edged sword.

These creators are free to say whatever they want without having to worry about traditional notions of journalistic decorum, but without institutional support from editors and fact-checkers, the quality of their output can suffer. It’s easy to fall into a ‘publish first, ask questions later’ mindset. According to UNESCO, a whopping [62% of creators don’t verify the accuracy of the material they share](https://edition.cnn.com/2024/11/26/media/social-media-influencers-verify-information-study/index.html) with their followers.

A tough landscape is getting even tougher
-----------------------------------------

Over the last decade or so, it has often felt like the media has lurched from one crisis to another, and the Reuters Institute’s report shows that 2025 will present yet more challenges.

*   An uptick in official hostility is making it harder for legacy media outlets to do their job.
*   Changes to search are affecting the visibility of their content.
*   While AI can be beneficial since it can bring in licensing revenue and promote efficiency in the newsroom, the benefits aren’t always shared equally.
*   The creatorification of media can emphasize style over substance.

These challenges underscore the need for media organizations to adapt and innovate in order to survive and thrive. While the road ahead may be fraught with obstacles, there are also opportunities for growth and reinvention.

--- SOURCE 4: 14.1 Evolving business models in journalism - Fiveable ---
URL: https://library.fiveable.me/introduction-journalism/unit-14/evolving-business-models-journalism/study-guide/az3zFCUmKZ2mhbZR

14.1 Evolving business models in journalism
===========================================

Citation:

MLA

Digital journalism faces major shifts as traditional revenue sources decline. News outlets now explore diverse income streams, from digital ads to paywalls. These changes aim to sustain quality journalism in an era of fragmented audiences and fierce online competition.

Paywalls and membership programs offer potential solutions. By restricting access and providing exclusive benefits, news organizations hope to build loyal readerships. However, balancing free and paid content while delivering value remains a challenge in this evolving landscape.

*   Decline of print advertising revenue due to advertisers shifting to online platforms (Google Ads, Facebook Ads) and decreased circulation of print newspapers and magazines
*   Disruption of traditional distribution channels with the rise of online news consumption (news websites, social media) and decreased reliance on physical distribution networks (newsstands, home delivery)
*   Fragmentation of audience attention caused by increased competition from digital media outlets (blogs, podcasts) and difficulty in maintaining reader loyalty and engagement

### Revenue streams for digital journalism

*   Advertising 
    *   Display ads shown on news websites and apps
    *   Native advertising designed to blend in with editorial content
    *   Sponsored content created in partnership with advertisers

*   Subscriptions 
    *   Paywalls restricting access to content for non-subscribers
    *   Premium content access granted to subscribers
    *   Bundled subscriptions with other services (streaming platforms, e-commerce)

*   Membership programs 
    *   Exclusive content and benefits for members (ad-free experience, newsletter)
    *   Community engagement and events (Q&A sessions, meetups)
    *   Donations and patron support to fund journalism

*   E-commerce and affiliate marketing 
    *   Product recommendations and reviews generating sales
    *   Commissions from partner retailers for referred purchases

*   Syndication and licensing 
    *   Selling content to other media outlets (wire services, content aggregators)
    *   Licensing content for use in other platforms (documentaries, books)

### Sustainability of digital business models

*   Advertising-based models face challenges in generating sufficient revenue and potential conflicts of interest and impact on editorial integrity
*   Subscription-based models struggle with acquiring and retaining subscribers and need high-quality, exclusive content to justify subscription fees
*   Membership-based models rely on a loyal and engaged audience and have potential for long-term sustainability through community support
*   Hybrid models combine multiple revenue streams and diversify income sources to mitigate risks

Paywalls and Membership Programs
--------------------------------

### Paywalls and subscription programs

*   Paywalls restrict access to content for non-subscribers 
    *   Metered paywalls allow limited free access (10 articles per month)
    *   Hard paywalls require subscription for all content

*   Subscriptions provide access to premium content and features and generate recurring revenue from loyal readers 
    *   Different subscription tiers and pricing options (basic, premium, annual)

*   Membership programs foster a sense of community and engagement and provide exclusive benefits and experiences for members 
    *   Encourage reader support through donations and patronage

*   Benefits and challenges 
    *   Potential for stable and predictable revenue streams
    *   Balancing subscriber acquisition and retention
    *   Maintaining a balance between free and paid content
    *   Ensuring the value proposition for subscribers and members (exclusive content, ad-free experience)

Key Terms to Review (26)
------------------------

[Fragmented audiences](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/fragmented-audiences): Fragmented audiences refer to the division of viewers, readers, or listeners into smaller, distinct groups due to the proliferation of media options and personalized content consumption. This phenomenon has emerged as traditional media outlets face challenges in maintaining a unified audience, impacting advertising strategies and content creation. As audiences become more specialized and dispersed, media organizations must adapt their business models to effectively engage with these niche markets.

[Audience Attention](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/audience-attention): Audience attention refers to the level of focus and engagement that a viewer, reader, or listener devotes to media content. It is crucial for journalists and content creators, as capturing and maintaining this attention influences how effectively a message is conveyed and received. In the context of journalism, understanding audience attention is vital for developing content strategies that resonate with audiences and drive interaction, especially as media consumption habits evolve.

[Hard paywalls](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/hard-paywalls): Hard paywalls are strict barriers placed by online publications that require readers to subscribe or pay a fee to access their content. Unlike soft paywalls, which allow limited access to articles before prompting for payment, hard paywalls restrict all access, meaning that visitors cannot view any content without paying. This model has become more common as news organizations seek sustainable revenue streams amid declining advertising income.

[Pricing options](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/pricing-options): Pricing options refer to the various strategies and models that media organizations use to charge consumers for their content, services, or products. This concept is crucial in the context of evolving business models in journalism as traditional revenue streams like advertising are declining, forcing news organizations to explore different methods to monetize their content. As journalism shifts towards more digital platforms, understanding how to effectively implement pricing options can significantly impact sustainability and revenue generation.

[Subscription tiers](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/subscription-tiers): Subscription tiers refer to the different levels of membership offered by news organizations, each providing varying access to content, services, and benefits. These tiers allow publishers to cater to diverse audiences by offering a range of pricing options and exclusive features, thereby creating new revenue streams while adapting to the changing landscape of journalism. By implementing subscription tiers, organizations can engage their audience more effectively and maximize their profitability in a competitive environment.

[Donations](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/donations): Donations refer to voluntary contributions of money or resources provided to support journalism organizations, initiatives, or specific projects. In the context of evolving business models in journalism, donations are becoming increasingly important as traditional revenue streams, such as advertising and subscriptions, decline. By fostering a culture of giving among audiences and supporters, journalism can adapt to financial challenges and sustain its operations in a rapidly changing media landscape.

[Metered paywalls](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/metered-paywalls): Metered paywalls are a type of subscription model used by digital news outlets that allows users to access a certain number of articles for free within a specific time frame, after which they are required to subscribe for continued access. This model aims to balance the need for revenue through subscriptions while still providing potential readers a taste of the content, which can encourage them to become paying subscribers. It reflects an evolving approach in journalism as organizations adapt to changing consumer habits and economic pressures.

[Licensing](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/licensing): Licensing refers to the process of granting permission to use intellectual property, such as trademarks, copyrights, or patents, under specific conditions. In journalism, licensing has become crucial as media organizations adapt to evolving business models, allowing them to monetize content while protecting their intellectual property rights and maintaining control over how their work is distributed and used.

[Reader loyalty](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/reader-loyalty): Reader loyalty refers to the commitment and attachment that an audience develops towards a particular news organization or publication. It is characterized by consistent engagement, such as repeated consumption of content and a preference for specific sources over others. This loyalty is crucial for journalism's evolving business models, as it influences subscription rates, advertising revenue, and overall sustainability in a competitive media landscape.

[Patron support](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/patron-support): Patron support refers to financial backing or funding provided by individuals, organizations, or institutions to sustain journalistic endeavors. This type of support plays a crucial role in the evolving landscape of journalism, especially as traditional revenue streams like advertising and subscriptions decline. By relying on patrons, journalists can maintain independence, produce high-quality content, and pursue investigative stories without being beholden to commercial interests.

[Hybrid models](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/hybrid-models): Hybrid models in journalism refer to business structures that combine traditional and digital revenue strategies to sustain news organizations. These models incorporate various income sources, like subscriptions, advertising, and donations, blending them to create a diverse financial ecosystem. This approach allows journalism to adapt to changing media landscapes while meeting the demands of audiences for both quality content and accessibility.

[Digital journalism](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/digital-journalism): Digital journalism refers to the practice of reporting news and information through digital platforms, utilizing the internet and multimedia tools to reach audiences. This form of journalism encompasses various formats, including text, video, audio, and interactive content, allowing for more engaging storytelling and immediate updates. As technology evolves, digital journalism continues to adapt in how it covers events, engages with the public, and generates revenue.

[Click-through rate](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/click-through-rate): Click-through rate (CTR) is a metric that measures the ratio of users who click on a specific link to the total number of users who view a page, email, or advertisement. It helps gauge the effectiveness of online content in attracting engagement and indicates how well a piece of digital media captures audience interest. A higher CTR often reflects more compelling content, which is crucial for both online writing and sustainable journalism business models.

[Vertical integration](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/vertical-integration): Vertical integration is a business strategy where a company expands its operations by acquiring or merging with other companies along the supply chain, from production to distribution. This approach allows companies to control multiple stages of the production process, reducing costs and increasing efficiency. In the context of journalism, vertical integration can impact how news organizations manage content creation, distribution, and advertising sales, ultimately influencing their business models.

[Return on Investment](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/return-on-investment): Return on Investment (ROI) is a financial metric used to evaluate the efficiency and profitability of an investment relative to its cost. In journalism, ROI helps media organizations measure the effectiveness of various business models and revenue streams, guiding them in making informed decisions to maximize profits and minimize risks.

[Hyperlocal news](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/hyperlocal-news): Hyperlocal news refers to news coverage focused on a specific community, neighborhood, or locality, often emphasizing local events, issues, and concerns. This type of journalism aims to provide detailed and relevant information to residents, connecting them to their immediate surroundings and fostering a sense of community involvement.

[Freemium model](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/freemium-model): The freemium model is a business strategy that offers basic services for free while charging a premium for advanced features or functionalities. This approach allows users to access core content without any initial cost, encouraging wider user adoption and potentially leading to conversion into paying customers for enhanced offerings. It is commonly used in digital media and software industries, allowing companies to monetize their products while building a user base.

[Audience monetization](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/audience-monetization): Audience monetization refers to the strategies and methods used by media organizations to generate revenue from their audience or readership. This concept is crucial as it connects various business models in journalism, allowing organizations to sustain their operations while providing valuable content to their audience. By leveraging audience data and engagement, companies can create targeted offerings that enhance their profitability while ensuring quality journalism remains accessible.

[NPR](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/npr): NPR, or National Public Radio, is a non-profit media organization in the United States that produces and distributes news and cultural programming. It aims to provide high-quality journalism and in-depth analysis, often filling gaps left by commercial media outlets. NPR operates under a different business model, relying on funding from listeners, grants, and corporate sponsors rather than solely on advertising revenue.

[Native advertising](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/native-advertising): Native advertising refers to a form of paid media that matches the form and function of the platform on which it appears, seamlessly blending in with the content to provide a more organic user experience. This technique aims to engage users by delivering promotional content that is relevant and non-disruptive, making it more likely for audiences to interact with it. Unlike traditional ads, native ads are designed to look and feel like regular content, which helps to build trust and reduce ad fatigue.

[Paywall](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/paywall): A paywall is a digital barrier that restricts access to online content unless a user pays for a subscription or one-time fee. It has become an essential component of evolving business models in journalism, as news organizations seek to monetize their content in a landscape where traditional revenue sources, like advertising, have diminished significantly.

[Media consolidation](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/media-consolidation): Media consolidation refers to the process where progressively fewer individuals or organizations control increasing shares of the mass media. This trend has significant implications for the diversity of voices and viewpoints available to the public, as well as the overall quality and integrity of journalism. As media companies merge or are acquired, they can prioritize profits over journalistic standards, leading to a homogenization of content and reduced local coverage.

[The New York Times](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/the-new-york-times): The New York Times is a prominent American newspaper known for its in-depth reporting, investigative journalism, and comprehensive coverage of global events. It has played a crucial role in shaping public opinion and setting standards in journalism, especially as media landscapes evolve and adapt to new business models and digital formats.

[Crowdfunding](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/crowdfunding): Crowdfunding is the practice of raising small amounts of money from a large number of people, typically via the internet, to fund projects or ventures. This method leverages the collective efforts of individuals and online platforms to pool resources for creative, entrepreneurial, or charitable initiatives, making it a popular alternative to traditional funding sources like banks or venture capitalists.

[Advertising model](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/advertising-model): The advertising model is a business strategy used by media organizations, where content is offered to audiences for free or at a low cost, while revenue is generated through the sale of advertising space. This model relies on attracting large audiences to maximize the value of advertisements placed within the content. It's particularly significant in the evolving landscape of journalism, as media outlets seek to adapt to changing consumer behaviors and technological advancements.

[Subscription model](https://library.fiveable.me/key-terms/introduction-journalism/subscription-model): The subscription model is a business strategy where customers pay a recurring fee, typically monthly or annually, to access a product or service. In journalism, this model has gained traction as traditional advertising revenues decline, allowing media organizations to create sustainable revenue streams by directly engaging their audience and providing exclusive content.

Capstone Franziska Schwarzmann Candidate for the Master of Arts in Law and Diplomacy, class of 2015 Student ID: 1146514 2 Table of Contents Introduction and Research Question 4 Literature Review 6 Theoretical Approach 10 Data Description and Analysis 14 Conclusion 25 3 Introduction and Research Question Print news in Europe is no longer profitable. Advertising expenditures declined from 2009 to 2013 by 20%.1 In the same period, the total number of paid-for daily newspapers in these countries fell from 2130 to 1315. In only 4 years, 40 percent of the newspapers have either consolidated or quit the market. Yet, democracies need news. Even though not officially, journalism is commonly referred to as the fourth pillar of a democratic system, practicing control over the branches of government, informing citizens.2 Digital delivery and the instant availability of news online have disrupted the print news model- a business model that has worked for the newspaper industry for almost a century. As a result, traditional newspapers are struggling to come up with economically sustainable digital business models, while non-traditional market entrants, mostly from the digital technology industry, capture market share.3 How to make money with their digital offerings is the key question for newspapers. Although there is a body of literature regarding digital content, delivery and pricing, there is a gap in the research when it comes to determining the success factors of digital newspapers. Current research has been anchored in the thought patterns of the old business model. But what is needed now is unrestricted thinking into different potential revenue channels. In this paper I have set out to make a contribution to thought leadership on digital newspaper business models by asking what factors determine a successful digital newspaper. In particular, I am focusing on a certain feature that online news content offers: interactivity. Online news, compared to print news, provides opportunities for the reader. Online, the reader has the option4 to become active once the content has been consumed. Possible activities include to give feedback on content immediately, to speak to others about it or to easily share content via social 1 Calculations from the WAN-Ifra report, calculated by author. http://www.wptdatabase.org/. 2 Donica Mensing, “Online Revenue Business Model Has Changed Little since 1996,” Newspaper Research Journal 28, no. 2 (Spring 2007): 33, accessed November 8, 2014. 3 There is an active debate between traditional media players and digital companies that want to enter the market. Representatives of the former like David Pemsel, Guardian News & Medie chief executive, emphasize how long they’ve been around, thus implying that they know the business best: “When I have been on panels with BuzzFeed and others, they sort of try and say ‘the industry of which we are a part’. Some of those platforms have been around for three years and we’ve been around for 200. They try and step into our space.”(Robert Andrews, “Old Media’s Prospects Have Improved but the New Media Threat Looms Large,” The Guardian, accessed March 22, 2015, http://www.theguardian.com/media-network/2015/mar/20/new-media-threat-buzzfeed-vice.) On the other hand are massively venture backed, digitally obsessed start-ups like “briefme”, an app that ranks news according to their spread within social media. (https://i-lab.harvard.edu/venture-incubation/resident-teams/briefme). 4 Ester de Waal, Online News: Uses and Effects of News Websites, 2007, 245, http://dare.uva.nl/document/51514. 4 media or email. With time, there has been a social aspect added to reading the news and consumption patterns have changed in such a way that readers expect content to be interactive. This is why I have hypothesized that content interactivity has become a necessary condition for the success of newspapers. My hypothesis is that there is a positive statistically significant correlation between interactive website features and the success of journalism business models, represented by the amount of unique monthly users to a website. What will I be able to observe if my hypothesis holds true? The more interactive features on the website, the more unique monthly visitors a newspaper has. While I do acknowledge that there are multiple factors contributing the amount of unique monthly visitors such as content and advertising, I have tried controlling for some factors by limiting my sample to national daily and paid for newspapers from Europe. There will be a positive significant correlation between interactive website feature and unique monthly visitors. What will I be able to observe if my thesis does not hold true? There might be newspaper websites with high unique monthly visitors that do not have many interactive features on their websites. Second, there might be newspaper websites with a low number of unique monthly visitors and many interactive features. The hypothesis is neither proven nor disproven if there is a low number of unique monthly visitors and a low account of interactive website features. In order to answer the question, I self-designed and gathered a dataset with indicators measuring interactivity as well as standard indicators that I want to control for. I looked at the national daily paid for newspapers in Germany, Austria, Switzerland and the United Kingdom, summing up to a total of 24 newspapers. I reasoned these indicators to be necessary to indicate whether a website offering can be categorized as interactive, based on existing research and my reasoning. With the data set I have put together, it is possible to distinguish between the possible answers. That being said, I have included a section on learning that will be helpful for future research to gather more comprehensive data. I will first lay out the state of current literature on the topic of digital journalism business models. Next, I will explain my analytical framework and then turn to the structure of my data set and what questions it can answer. Finally, I include how my contribution to digital journalism research can be further used in the academic community as well as thought leadership in the industry. 5 Literature Review As key to further development of online news describes Richard van der Wurff the question of business models and revenue sources. 5 According to him, the internet offers a plethora of choices to publishers. His study “Print and Online Newspapers in Europe” gives an overview over what types of internet-specific features are offered by online newspapers and in what proportions. His definition and operationalization of interactivity are the basis for my data collection, because he distinguishes between interpersonal and content interactivity, whereby the former means that the reader can interact with other individuals and the latter implies that the reader can interact with the content. In addition to that, van der Wurff tests all newspaper websites for specific features on one day. An approach that I have used for my data collection as well. While van der Wurff’s research design and his operationalization serve in part as the basis for this paper’s operationalization and data gathering design, his book lacks a perspective: He only offers a theory in which online news is an addition to newspapers. His hypothesis is that the internet potentially functions as three different – complementary to print - channels, (1) the internet as a marketing channel, driving engagement with the customer (2) as an additional distribution channel to long- distance audiences or (3) a channel offering additional multimedia services for the core print audience. 6 Just as van der Wurff, Robert Picard has offered research that considers online as an extension of print. Robert Picard’s argument is the basis for my consideration that besides content there are other success factors of online journalism. He stresses that online has not triggered a revolution of content, but rather a revolution of content distribution and use. He argues that the success of online content is contingent upon obtaining and maintaining usage by offering products and services that understand the altered behavior of individuals and society. The basis for his thoughts is that there is a priori demand for a product; and new technologies offer a service extension to already existing customers: “Many believe the rhetoric that new and unique products and services are being created that will significantly alter the behavior of individuals and society. In reality, most of these products and services are just creating faster, easier, and more flexible means for consumers to do what they are already doing.” 7 The reader is given more choice and this 5 Richard van der Wurff and Edmund Lauf, Print and Online Newspapers in Europe. A Comparative Analysis in 16 Countries (Het Spinhuis Publishers, Amsterdam, 2005), chap. 2. 6 Richard van der Wurff and Edmund Lauf, Print and Online Newspapers in Europe. A Comparative Analysis in 16 Countries (Het Spinhuis Publishers, Amsterdam, 2005), chap. 2. 7 Robert G. Picard, “Changing Business Models of Online Content Services : Their Implications for Multimedia and Other Content Producers,” International Journal on Media Management 2, no. 2 (January 2000): 61. Comment [T1]: This sentence does not make sense 6 choice alters the relationship between the user and media. New methods of content presentation combined with technologies enabling more flexible use give an advantage to the consumer, for which he is willing to pay. However, the customer’s willingness to pay is a result of a perceived value added.8 Picard’s focus is on online content services. Thus, he does not give any examples of what this perceived value add could look like in the newspaper sphere. This is where van der Wurff’s research sets in: When van der Wurff published his study in 2004, he introduced a dichotomized way of thinking about the internet as a news medium: He said the internet could either be leveraged to improve the incumbent business model by improving secondary functions or the internet could be leveraged to transform a business model. Van der Wurff suggests that as soon as news is published online, there is an internet-specific feature added to a business model: Interactivity in content and interpersonally, multimediacy and immediacy. The definition of interactivity is highly contextual. Technologically, interactivity can be defined as “the extent to which users can participate in modifying the form and content of a mediated environment in real-time”. 9 There are also more journalism-specific definitions; they all have the common notion that digital journalism can empower the audience. These definitions differ regarding their sub-categorizations of interactivity: Communication researcher Jensen argues for four subconcepts of interactivity, Deuze categorizes three levels and Liu and Shrum focus on structural versus perceived interactivity. 10 In 2004, Bucy distinguished two types of interactivity: content interactivity, meaning consumers exercise control over editorial content and interpersonal interactivity, meaning real-time or delayed person-to-person conversations mediated by the news site.11 For the purposes of this paper, Bucy’s categorization makes the most sense as the simple dichotomized version of interactivity fits my research design the most. For years, interactivity has received praise as one of the key factors of digital journalism. In 2001, Deuze argued that interactivity is the added value of online journalism, J.D. Lasica claimed in 1998: “Online news publications have been so swept away by the graphic grandeur of the Web that they've all 8 Ibid. 9 Jens F. Jensen, “‘Interactivity’. Tracking a New Concept in Media and Communication Studies,” Nordicom Review 19, no. 1 (1998): 201. 10 Mark Deuze, “The Internet and Its Journalisms - Part I: A Typology of Online Journalism,” Online Journalism Review, July 11, 2002, 84, www.ojr.org/ojr/future/p1026407729.php; Deuze, “The Internet and Its Journalisms - Part I: A Typology of Online Journalism”; Yuping Liu and L.J. Shrum, “What Is Interactivity and Is It Always a Good Thing? Implications of Definition, Person, and Situation for the Influence of Interactivity on Advertising Effectiveness,” Journal of Advertising 31, no. 4 (2002): 55–64. 11 Erik Bucy, “Second Generation Net News: Interactivity and Information Accessibility in the Online Environment,” International Journal on Media Management 6, no. 1–2 (2004): 102–13. 7 but ignored the truly revolutionary promise of the Internet: interactivity.” 12 Content interactivity is the answer Vin Crosbie gives regarding the question of how to design a successful newspaper business model. In 2004, Vin Crosbie argued for content as well as channel customization, suggesting that readers needed to be able to decide what kind of news they received. In his opinion, the newspaper industry had wasted its energy on the wrong means and should have concentrated on creating more interactive papers tailored to readers’ individual interests. He stresses that declining readership numbers as well as declining willingness to pay may mostly be attributed to the lack of individual relevancy. The world’s first newspaper published stories that the editors anticipated would be of the greatest generic interest and this decision had been due to the technical limitations of printing, Crosbie continues. “Nearly four subsequent centuries of new printing technologies have increased the speed and image resolutions of analog presses, but that generic limitation has remained.”13 Reasoning that on average a reader is not interested in more than four articles of a generic newspaper, Crosbie concludes that this represents a “lousy satisfaction ratio”.14 Just as the invention of the transistor had initiated a revolution which enabled readers to choose a mix of media that best satisfied each of their own unique preferences, so should interactivity shatter that antiquated limitation. The industry, Crosbie continued, shall (1) use new technologies in order to match reader’s interests, (2) be more courageous to think out of channel silos and vision a truly converged product, (3) not limit itself to being a content producing machine, but instead deliver heroes of a content package. Even though his article in the Journalism Review is a solid analysis, it lacks any sort of academic justification because he presents his opinion, but does not prove that the generic limitations and reporters stuck in the culture thereof are to be blamed for the demise of newspapers. Interestingly, Ester de Waal produces an argument countering Crosbie’s suggestions. Her findings suggest that while generic print newspapers were good at making people read what they would otherwise not have read, online newspapers are less successful with this. 15 This sample of the research on interactivity suggests that it is not clear whether interactivity is a success factor for online news and thus far, this question has not been the subject of research. 12 Mark Deuze, “Online Journalism: Modelling the First Generation of News Media on the World Wide Web,” First Monday 6, no. 10, accessed November 28, 2015, http://firstmonday.org/ojs/index.php/fm/article/view/893; J.D. Lasica, “A Great Way to Strengthen Bonds. Online News Publications Should Take Better Advantage of the Net’s Interactivity.,” American Journalism Review, March 1998, http://ajrarchive.org/Article.asp?id=1789. 13 Vin Crosbie, “What Newspapers and Their Web Sites Must Do to Survive,” USC Annenberg Journalism Review, 2004, http://www.ojr.org/ojr/business/1078349998.php. 14 Ibid. 15 Ester de Waal, “Online and Print Newspapers: Their Impact on the Extent of the Perceived Public Agenda,” in Online News: Uses and Effects of News Websites, 2007, 69, http://dare.uva.nl/document/51514. 8 Customization of distribution channels is a factor for attracting younger target groups, according to the Media Insight Project. Their study about how millennials consume news found that “Millennials also appear to be drawn into news that they might otherwise have ignored because peers are recommending and contextualizing it for them on social networks, as well as on more private networks such as group texts and instant messaging.”16 88% of the respondents answered that they visited Facebook in order to get news. Why there is such a low pricing for online news has been the focus of a paper by Cunningham and Compaine. They found that online newspapers were offered for free because the early online subscription ideas failed and readers were not willing to pay. So the "free" model emerged, with the expectation - the hope - that advertisers would pay to reach the larger numbers that will click when the price is zero. Today, publishers are dealing with that legacy. Advertising revenues have grown, but not as fast as the revenue base for the printed newspaper has evaporated."17 One reason why online journalism has not been successful can be attributed to the fact that “newspaper managers as a whole do not appear to have altered their online strategies significantly”18, concludes Donica Mensing. The old business model for the print newspaper had two revenue sources, advertising and subscription. Newspapers have mainly tried to alter their ways around these revenue streams. Mensing attests that “despite massive changes in the market for news and aggressive competition for advertising revenue, newspaper managers as a whole do not appear to have altered their online strategies significantly during this nine year period.”19 The success of online newspapers has been looked at from different perspectives: In comparison to print editions, measured in its ability to generate revenue by attracting subscribers. However, what is not existent in the current literature is an analysis looking at what makes newspapers successful without directly linking success to monetization. 16 “How Millennials Get News,” American Press Institute, accessed March 22, 2015, http://www.americanpressinstitute.org/publications/reports/survey-research/millennials-news/. 17 Benjamin Compaine and Brendan Cunningham, “Addressing Real Issues for the Uncertain and Challenging Environment of the Media Industry,” Journal of Media Economics 23, no. 3 (August 31, 2010): 112. 18 Donica Mensing, “Online Revenue Business Model Has Changed Little since 1996,” Newspaper Research Journal 28, no. 2 (Spring 2007): 22, accessed November 8, 2014. 19 Ibid., 33. 9 Theoretical Approach My hypothesis is that there is a positive statistically significant correlation between interactive website features and the success of journalism business models, represented by the amount of unique monthly users to a website. Why? I base this hypothesis on two trends: The demand for print papers has been decreasing, and traffic to news sites depends on unique content as well as repeated visits. And “winning sites are those that are more convenient, user friendly, better organized and more visually appealing”.20 Interactivity has been described as the key element of economic viability for Web news organizations21, yet academically it has not been subject of research whether and if yes, what kind of interactivity is the secret sauce to news site traffic. Independent Variables I have operationalized interactivity with a subset of indicators which I will discuss in this section. This research is mainly based on van der Wurf’s framework of operationalization. The dimensions of interactivity are based on Bucy. Additionally, I have chosen 20 national daily, paid-for newspaper websites from four countries which I will introduce in this section. Content Interactivity Customization of Channels and of Content Donica Mensing notices two trends, but does not make a connection between them. First, from 1996 until 2005, advertising became more important as a source of revenue and concurrently more fragile due to online competition. 22Second, customization services can become a new source of revenue. This thesis follows the hypothesis that customization, hence the ability from a readers perspective to interact with content as well as the producer, reinforces advertising as a revenue channel, because once you understand your customer’s preference, you can target the advertising much more efficiently. This is especially in line with Mensing’s conclusion that “while advertising may generate sufficient revenue to keep some or many online newspapers operating, it appears they will need significantly to accelerate the scale and scope of this revenue source if they are to thrive successfully in a market that is fundamentally different from the market print newspapers have grown to dominate in 20 Paschalia-Lia Spyridou and Andreas Veglis, “Exploring Structural Interactivity in Online Newspapers: A Look at the Greek Landscape,” Journal Article (University of Illinois at Chicago University Library, April 28, 2008), http://firstmonday.org/article/view/2164/1960. 21 Ibid. 22 Donica Mensing, “Online Revenue Business Model Has Changed Little since 1996,” Newspaper Research Journal 28, no. 2 (Spring 2007): 22, accessed November 8, 2014. 10 many communities.”23 I see customization as a subset of interactivity, because it enables the reader to become active and choose that channel or content that she prefers.  Customization of Channels: Does the newspaper offer its content via different channels? I observe different channels (Email, Ebook, Smartphone, Tablet, Website) in order to understand what can be seen as standard and what might make a difference. This is a dichotomized variable, with the answer being either yes or no for each channel.  Customization of Content: Can the reader get individualized content packages? This is a dichotomized variable, with the answer being either yes or no.  Interactivity of content: Does the newspaper offer more content on the website in the form of a search engine, hyperlinks or an archive? I have included archives because Donica Mensing found in 2007 that “the revenue from archives, while very small, provided measurable income in 1996 and 2005.” From an interactive perspective, archives give the reader an opportunity to Hyperlinks are an option because Harvard’s Nieman Lab called them web journalism’s “currency of collaboration”24. Interpersonal Interactivity Social Media  Social Media: Indicates whether a newspaper has a Twitter channel. This is a dichotomized variable, with the answer being yes or no. I realize that there are several ways to operationalize Social Media and several social media channels like Facebook or Pinterest that matter. I have decided to go with Twitter as an indicator for journalism, because Twitter is a platform that has been knowingly used for the distribution and social dissemination of news.25 The presence of newspapers in social media channels offers several opportunities for interactivity. Social media is also way to attract new readers.27 How much of a role social media plays in today’s journalism is already reflected in the curricula of journalism MA programs.28  Interpersonal Interactivity: Does the newspaper offer the opportunity for the reader to engage with the author or other readers? I have observed whether there is an opportunity to get directly in touch with the author, whether there is a commenting function underneath the article, whether the newspaper publishes letter to the editor on the website, whether the newspaper provisions a chatroom or a discussion forum and whether there is a sharing function for each article.  Digital Payment Method I included Digital Payment Methods because giving the reader the opportunity to pay as she wishes, and wherever, is a part of a customized and interactive offering. Whereas many millennials subscribe to paid services for movies, music and television and claim to pay for it themselves, they seem reluctant to pay for digital news. In-depth qualitative interviews revealed the sentiment valuing news as a universal right for which one should not have to pay. Without a doubt, a newspaper needs readers. Readers are a necessary condition for the success of a newspaper. However, a viable future business model might not depend on a consumer’s willingness to pay for news. Thus, readers might not be a sufficient condition for the monetary success of newspapers. Since the industry as a whole seems stuck and unable to answer the question of monetization, I wanted to choose a dependent variable on whose basis new research and ideas could be leveraged, thus my dependent variable is “unique monthly users”. The dependent variable, monthly unique visitors, comes from the World Association of Newspaper’s World Press Trends database. Scope In my selection of newspapers, I have chosen all national daily paid for newspapers in Germany, Austria, Switzerland and the United Kingdom. I limited this selection to those countries for which language does not produce a barrier to me. While all countries are European, Austria, Germany and Switzerland comprise one cultural region within Europe while the United Kingdom represents another language and a culture that stands in contrast to the German speaking region. Additionally, the UK, Germany and Austria are bound by policies of the European Union, Switzerland is not. enriched_points.expanded_content: Digital journalism must swiftly adapt to technological advancements, audience preferences, and market dynamics. Innovations like AI and interactive content are crucial for engaging fragmented audiences effectively.

Ethical journalism is vital to maintain trust amid misinformation. News organizations must prioritize transparency, accountability, and accurate reporting to foster credibility in the digital landscape.

enriched_points.point_title: Focus on Audience Needs and Trust
enriched_points.expanded_content: Understanding audience preferences enhances engagement. News outlets should build trust through personalized content, responsive communication, and community engagement to sustain loyalty and relevance.
